reason:
A_k is never precisely defined. The statement refers to 'a finitely-presented *-algebra A_k' but does not specify its generators or relations. Several interpretations are possible:

(a) A_k = the free *-algebra on the operator variables modulo the measurement relations (projector, normalization, commutation). This is infinite-dimensional for any k, and one needs to verify that the main theorem (Node 1) applies to it. In particular, one must check that A_k^{sa} is an Archimedean order unit space, which requires a C*-norm or similar structure. The free *-algebra modulo polynomial relations is generally NOT a C*-algebra and may fail to be Archimedean unless one takes its C*-enveloping algebra.

(b) A_k = some finite-dimensional matrix algebra related to the moment matrix at level k. In this case it is simply M_N(C) for some N, and the *-algebra language adds nothing beyond Node 1.6.

(c) A_k = the truncated algebra of words of length ≤ k with some multiplication rule. But truncated algebras of words are not algebras under concatenation (concatenation of two words of length k gives a word of length 2k, which is outside the algebra).

The node must define A_k precisely and verify that it is a unital *-algebra satisfying the hypotheses of the main theorem. Without this, the claim 'feasible points correspond to states φ on A_k' is not a well-formed mathematical statement.
